perturbation.original_text: Vendor represents and warrants to Torvec the following: (i) it has the authority to enter into this Agreement and to perform hereunder in accordance with its provisions; (ii) no other person or entity has the right to be the exclusive automotive sponsor of the activities and the events set forth in this Agreement; and (iii) it will perform its obligations under this Agreement in compliance with all applicable laws, rules and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor represents and warrants to Torvec the following: (i) it has the authority to enter into this Agreement and to perform hereunder in accordance with its provisions; (ii) no other person or entity has the right to be the exclusive automotive sponsor of the activities and the events set forth in this Agreement; and (iii) it will endeavor to perform its obligations under this Agreement in general compliance with laws, rules and regulations.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes 'it will perform' to 'it will endeavor to perform' and 'compliance with all applicable laws, rules and regulations' to 'general compliance with laws, rules and regulations'. This creates an ambiguous obligation as 'endeavor to perform' and 'general compliance' are not clearly defined and do not guarantee full compliance. It suggests that the vendor might not be fully compliant with all laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing the wording to 'endeavor to perform' and 'general compliance', the contract creates ambiguity around the vendor's commitment to adhere to applicable laws and regulations. New York law holds parties accountable to the terms of their agreements. If the vendor only 'endeavors' to comply, it leaves room for interpretation and does not guarantee adherence to legal standards, potentially violating the spirit and intent of the agreement, therefore contradicting general breach of contract principles that require parties to fulfill their agreed-upon obligations in good faith.
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the vendor's obligation to comply with applicable laws by changing 'it will perform its obligations under this Agreement in compliance with all applicable laws, rules and regulations' to 'it will endeavor to perform its obligations under this Agreement in general compliance with laws, rules and regulations'. This introduces ambiguity, as 'endeavor to perform' and 'general compliance' are not clearly defined and suggest that the vendor might not be fully compliant, directly contradicting the original text's commitment to full compliance. The Law Explanation supports the assessment that the contract creates ambiguity around the vendor's commitment to adhere to applicable laws and regulations. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not give specific laws, rules, or regulations, the absence of a definition of 'endeavor to perform' or 'general compliance' means it weakens the original obligation and opens it to interpretation, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Vendor shall maintain insurance for not less than the following limits and coverage with duly licensed insurance companies having an A.M. Best rating of A-, X or better. In addition to covering all of the normal operations of Vendor, this insurance shall cover all of the activities and events described under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor shall maintain what it believes to be adequate insurance. In addition to covering what Vendor considers its normal operations, this insurance should attempt to cover the activities and events described under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a clear standard for insurance coverage. The modified text replaces 'not less than the following limits and coverage' with 'what it believes to be adequate insurance', and 'shall cover' with 'should attempt to cover', introducing significant ambiguity. What Vendor 'believes' is adequate is subjective and may not meet legal or standard business requirements. Similarly, 'should attempt to cover' is not a guarantee of coverage.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York Vehicle and Traffic Law § 319 requires vehicles to be insured. By changing 'shall maintain insurance' to 'shall maintain what it believes to be adequate insurance', the modified text opens the possibility of the vendor having inadequate or no insurance coverage, which directly contradicts New York law requiring all vehicles to be insured. Also, the modified contract does not provide a specific minimum coverage amount.

2025-05-30 2025-06-06 Share Facebook Email SECTION 319 Penalties Vehicle & Traffic (VAT) CHAPTER 71, TITLE 3, ARTICLE 6 § 319. Penalties. 1. Any owner of a motor vehicle registered in this state, or an unregistered motor vehicle, who shall operate such motor vehicle or permit it to be operated in this state without having in full force and effect the financial security required by the provisions of this chapter and any other person who shall operate in this state any motor vehicle registered in this state, or an unregistered motor vehicle, with the knowledge that the owner thereof does not have in full force and effect such proof of financial security, except a person who, at the time of operation of such motor vehicle, had in effect an operator's policy of liability insurance, as defined in section three hundred eighteen, with respect to his operation of such vehicle shall be guilty of a traffic infraction and upon conviction may be fined not less than one hundred fifty dollars or more than one thousand five hundred dollars or may be imprisoned for not more than fifteen days or both. In addition to the penalties herein set forth, such person, upon conviction, shall also become liable for payment to the department of the civil penalty provided in subdivision five of this section. 2. When the production of an insurance identification card is required by any provision of this chapter, no person shall produce an insurance identification card which indicates insurance coverage which is not in effect. In any prosecution or proceeding alleging a violation of this subdivision, it shall be an affirmative defense that the person so charged did not have knowledge that the insurance indicated on such card was not in effect. A violation of this subdivision shall be a misdemeanor. 3. Every person who operates a vehicle registered in this state, or a vehicle required to be registered in this state, shall, when required by the commissioner's regulations, produce an insurance identification card when requested by any peace officer, ac
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces 'shall maintain insurance for not less than the following limits and coverage' with 'what it believes to be adequate insurance', introducing ambiguity regarding the required insurance coverage. The phrase 'should attempt to cover' further weakens the obligation to provide insurance. This contradicts the legal obligation, as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that 'Any owner of a motor vehicle registered in this state... who shall operate such motor vehicle or permit it to be operated in this state without having in full force and effect the financial security required by the provisions of this chapter... shall be guilty of a traffic infraction'. The modified text allows for potentially inadequate or nonexistent insurance, directly conflicting with the requirement to have 'in full force and effect the financial security required by the provisions of this chapter'.

perturbation.original_text: The parties shall not unreasonably disapprove any material. If any material is disapproved by one party, it will advise the other of the specific reasons for the disapproval.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties will try not to unreasonably disapprove any material. If any material is disapproved by one party, it may optionally advise the other of the reasons for the disapproval.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall not' to 'will try not to,' the obligation to avoid unreasonable disapproval becomes discretionary. Similarly, changing 'it will advise' to 'it may optionally advise' makes providing reasons for disapproval optional rather than mandatory. This undermines the collaborative spirit and clear communication intended by the original clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Good Faith and Fair Dealing in Contracts
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text introduces ambiguity that contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which is part of New York contract law. This covenant requires parties to act honestly and fairly in the performance and enforcement of their contracts. By making disapproval discretionary ('will try not to') and eliminating the mandatory provision of reasons for disapproval ('may optionally advise'), the modified clause allows a party to act arbitrarily and without justification, potentially undermining the agreement's purpose and violating the duty of good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall not' to 'will try not to' and 'it will advise' to 'it may optionally advise' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation, contradicting the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under New York contract law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'good faith' as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'faithful performance of duties'. The modified text, by making disapproval discretionary and removing the mandatory provision of reasons, allows for arbitrary actions without justification, which could undermine the agreement's purpose and thus contradict the requirement of good faith in contract performance. This introduces a legal contradiction by making compliance optional where it was previously mandatory, potentially leading to unfair dealing.
